title: Fortinet FortiClient EMS Critical Pre-Auth API Access Bypass (CVE-2026-35616, CVSS 9.1): Unauthenticated Attackers Forge Certificate Headers to Gain Full RCE on Endpoint Management Servers — CISA KEV with April 9 Federal Deadline, Emergency Hotfix Required

timeline.event: First active exploitation detected by watchTowr honeypot sensors; vulnerability used as zero-day in attacks
watchTowr Labs publicly discloses the zero-day and confirms active exploitation in the wild
Bishop Fox publishes technical analysis of the API authentication bypass mechanism
Fortinet releases emergency hotfixes for FortiClient EMS 7.4.5 and 7.4.6 (FG-IR-26-099); multiple security vendors publish advisories
CISA adds CVE-2026-35616 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog with binding federal remediation deadline
CISA BOD 22-01 federal remediation deadline — all Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies must have patched by this date

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical security flaw was found in Fortinet FortiClient EMS — the central management server that enterprises use to control security policies on all employee endpoints. The vulnerability allows attackers to bypass authentication entirely by forging HTTP certificate headers. The server's Django application backend never performs real cryptographic verification — it only checks whether the subject and issuer strings in the headers match, so an attacker can craft fake headers that satisfy this check. This means anyone on the network can send a specially crafted HTTP request and gain full administrative access to the EMS server as if they were a legitimate admin — no password needed. Active exploitation began on March 31, 2026, before Fortinet had a patch ready, making this a zero-day attack. CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with an urgent April 9 deadline for federal agencies.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running Fortinet FortiClient EMS versions 7.4.5 or 7.4.6 that is accessible over the network. Shadowserver identified over 2,000 publicly exposed FortiClient EMS instances worldwide. The version 7.2 branch is not affected. This is particularly serious because FortiClient EMS is used by enterprises to centrally manage endpoint security on thousands of workstations — compromising it gives attackers visibility into and control over all managed endpoints.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attackers scan the internet for FortiClient EMS instances exposed on the network. Step 2: They craft a malicious HTTP request with forged certificate header values that pass the EMS server's string-matching check — no valid certificate is actually needed. Step 3: The EMS API grants full authenticated access based on the fake headers, bypassing all login requirements. Step 4: Attackers execute arbitrary commands on the EMS server with full administrative privileges, potentially extracting endpoint telemetry, security policies, and stored credentials. Step 5: Using the compromised EMS as a staging point, attackers push malicious configurations to all managed endpoints, effectively gaining control over the entire endpoint fleet — potentially deploying ransomware or persistent backdoors across the organization.
what_you_should_do: Apply the emergency hotfix immediately: download the FG-IR-26-099 hotfix from the Fortinet FortiGuard portal for your version (7.4.5 or 7.4.6) and install it on all FortiClient EMS instances without delay — the CISA deadline is April 9
Restrict network access to the FortiClient EMS management API to trusted IP ranges only; block any external internet-facing exposure using firewall rules until the patch is confirmed applied

Review all EMS logs since March 31, 2026 for signs of exploitation — look for unexpected API calls, unauthorized configuration changes, or suspicious child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe) spawned from the EMS service
Investigate all managed endpoints for signs of lateral movement or malicious policy changes deployed from the compromised EMS server
Plan upgrade to FortiClient EMS 7.4.7 (the full patch release) once available
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: FortiClient EMS is the command center for endpoint security across an organization — it controls what security policies run on every managed workstation and server. Compromising it gives attackers both intelligence on the security posture of all endpoints and the ability to push malicious changes to every device simultaneously. This is the equivalent of gaining control of the IT security team's master control panel. The combination of pre-authentication access (no credentials needed), confirmed active exploitation in the wild since March 31, CISA emergency listing with a 3-day federal deadline, and over 2,000 exposed instances globally makes this one of the most urgent enterprise threats of April 2026.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the improper access control flaw in the FortiClient EMS API endpoint, bypassing authentication by forging HTTP certificate headers to gain initial access to the management server
After bypassing authentication, attackers execute arbitrary commands on the EMS server through the compromised API interface, spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe child processes
The authentication bypass grants immediate administrative-level access, effectively providing attacker-controlled privilege escalation without needing a separate exploitation step
Attackers abuse the EMS certificate validation shortcut (string matching without cryptographic verification) to masquerade as legitimate administrators
Compromised EMS server is used as a pivot point to push malicious configurations, policies, or tooling to all managed endpoints across the organization
Threat actors with full EMS control can deploy ransomware payloads to all managed endpoints simultaneously via the endpoint management infrastructure

detection_guidance.summary: Detection requires monitoring FortiClient EMS application logs for unauthorized API access, suspicious process spawning from the EMS service, and anomalous HTTP requests with crafted certificate headers. Correlate web access logs with downstream system activity to detect post-exploitation behavior.

log_sources.description: Monitor the EMS application log for API requests that succeed without a valid session context, unusual access to administrative endpoints, and any configuration change events not initiated by known administrators
Monitor for suspicious child processes spawned by the FortiClient EMS Windows service, particularly cmd.exe, powershell.exe, pwsh.exe, or wscript.exe; use Sysmon event ID 1 for process creation
Monitor inbound HTTP/HTTPS traffic to the EMS management port; flag requests with unusual or malformed certificate header fields; alert on connections from unexpected IP ranges
Review telemetry from managed endpoints for unexpected policy changes pushed from the EMS server after March 31, 2026, which may indicate attacker-controlled policy modification

what_to_look_for.indicator: Suspicious process spawned from EMS service
what_to_look_for.description: Detects post-exploitation command execution — the EMS service should never spawn interactive shells; presence of cmd.exe or PowerShell as a child process indicates successful RCE
HTTP 200 responses to FortiClient EMS API endpoints without a valid authenticated session cookie or token may indicate the bypass is being exploited

what_to_look_for.indicator: Forged certificate header in HTTP request
what_to_look_for.description: Requests containing non-standard SSL_CLIENT_CERT or similar certificate-forwarding headers from clients that have not completed a real TLS handshake with a valid certificate

what_to_look_for.indicator: Mass endpoint policy changes after March 31
what_to_look_for.description: Bulk configuration or policy updates pushed to managed endpoints after March 31, 2026, especially from unexpected admin accounts or at unusual hours

immediate_actions.description: Download and install the FG-IR-26-099 hotfix for FortiClient EMS 7.4.5 or 7.4.6 from the Fortinet FortiGuard support portal. The CISA BOD 22-01 federal deadline is April 9, 2026 — this is the most urgent patching action currently tracked by CISA.

immediate_actions.action: Block external network access to EMS management API
immediate_actions.description: Immediately restrict network access to FortiClient EMS to trusted internal IP ranges only. No EMS management interface should be reachable from the public internet. Implement firewall rules or ACLs to enforce this.

immediate_actions.action: Audit EMS logs for exploitation since March 31
immediate_actions.description: Review FortiClient EMS application and web access logs from March 31, 2026 onward for signs of unauthorized access, suspicious API calls, or unexpected admin actions. Check for child process spawning from the EMS service.

immediate_actions.action: Verify all managed endpoint policies for unauthorized changes
immediate_actions.description: Audit all endpoint security policies managed by FortiClient EMS for unauthorized modifications after March 31. Any policy changes not authorized by your team may indicate attacker tampering via the compromised EMS.

Enable IDS/IPS detection rules for CVE-2026-35616 on network security appliances protecting the EMS server. Monitor the Sigma rule targeting suspicious child process spawning from FortiClientEMS service.
